question: [File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestHandler.js -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: requestLogger()] Can an unprivileged external user flood the server with concurrent requests to exhaust the Node.js event loop during a DB network partition, causing `await randomString(8)` to queue indefinitely and block all subsequent middleware execution, resulting in a full service denial for all users?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestHandler.js -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: requestLogger()] Can an unprivileged external user send thousands of simultaneous requests during a DB partition event, causing `httpContext.set(requestIdLabel, requestId)` to be called across thousands of concurrent async contexts, exhausting the async local storage namespace and causing request context corruption or memory exhaustion?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestHandler.js -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: requestLogger()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger a network partition scenario by flooding requests such that `res.locals.statusCode = httpStatusCodes.OK.code` is set but the downstream DB call never completes, causing the response handler to emit HTTP 200 with empty or partial data to all queued clients when the partition resolves?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestHandler.js -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: requestLogger()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that `requestLogger` is `async` but does not call `next()` explicitly, so that if `await randomString(8)` hangs due to OS entropy depletion under high-load partition conditions, the entire middleware chain stalls indefinitely with no timeout, causing permanent request queue buildup?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestHandler.js -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: requestLogger()] Can an unprivileged external user send a sustained flood of requests to deplete the OS entropy pool used by `randomBytesAsync` inside `randomString(8)`, causing `requestLogger` to block on every new request during a network partition, amplifying the partition's impact beyond its natural duration?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestHandler.js -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: requestLogger()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of a circuit breaker in `requestLogger` so that during a network partition, every incoming request still allocates `res.locals`, sets `requestStartTime = Date.now()`, and enters the DB pool queue, exhausting the pool's `maxConnections: 10` and causing all subsequent requests to timeout at `connectionTimeout: 20000ms`, resulting in cascading 503 responses?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestHandler.js -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: requestLogger()] Can an unprivileged external user craft requests that cause `httpContext.set(requestIdLabel, requestId)` to overwrite a prior request's context in the same async continuation chain, leading to request ID collision and cross-request log correlation attacks that obscure the attacker's own requests during a partition event?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestHandler.js -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: requestLogger()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that `res.locals[requestStartTime] = Date.now()` is set at middleware entry but no timeout is enforced, so that during a network partition, requests accumulate in memory with their `res.locals` objects held alive, causing unbounded heap growth and eventual OOM crash?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestHandler.js -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: requestLogger()] Can an unprivileged external user send requests at a rate that causes `randomString(8)` async calls to pile up in the microtask queue during a partition, starving the event loop of I/O callbacks needed to detect the partition recovery, thereby extending the effective outage window beyond the actual partition duration?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestHandler.js -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: requestLogger()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the lack of request-level timeout in `requestLogger` so that during a network partition, the `requestStartTime` stored in `res.locals` becomes arbitrarily stale, causing the elapsed time logged in `responseHandler` to overflow or produce misleading metrics that mask the partition's true impact?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestHandler.js -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: requestQueryParser()] Can an unprivileged external user send a query string with exactly `config.query.maxRepeatedQueryParameters` (default 100) repetitions of a single key using mixed case variants (e.g., `ORDER=asc&Order=asc&...`), causing `qs.parse` to accept them under the `arrayLimit` but the post-parse `merge()` loop to combine them into a single key array that exceeds the limit, bypassing the `throwOnLimitExceeded` guard and creating an oversized array that exhausts downstream processing during a partition?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestHandler.js -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: requestQueryParser()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a query string with `N` distinct case-variant keys that all lowercase to the same key (e.g., `order`, `ORDER`, `Order`, `oRdEr`), causing the `merge()` function to be called `N-1` times and build an array of size `N` that is never checked against `maxRepeatedQueryParameters` because the limit check in `qs.parse` only applies per original key, not per lowercased key?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestHandler.js -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: requestQueryParser()] Can an unprivileged external user send a query string that causes `qs.parse(queryString, queryOptions)` to throw due to `throwOnLimitExceeded: true` when `arrayLimit` is exceeded, and if this exception is not caught within `requestQueryParser`, it propagates as an unhandled synchronous exception through Express's query parser hook (set via `app.set('query parser', requestQueryParser)`), potentially crashing the process or causing an unhandled rejection during a partition event?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestHandler.js -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: requestQueryParser()] Can an unprivileged external user send a deeply nested query string (e.g., `a[b][c]=1`) that violates `depth: 1` with `strictDepth: true`, causing `qs.parse` to throw a synchronous exception inside `requestQueryParser`, which is called as Express's query parser before any error middleware is registered, resulting in an unhandled exception that crashes the server process during a partition event when error handling is already degraded?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestHandler.js -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: requestQueryParser()] Can an unprivileged external user send a query string with `maxRepeatedQueryParameters + 1` repetitions of a key to trigger `throwOnLimitExceeded: true` in `qs.parse`, and since `requestQueryParser` has no try/catch, the thrown error bypasses the Express error handler registered later in the middleware chain, causing a 500 or process crash that is amplified during a network partition when the server is already under stress?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestHandler.js -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: requestQueryParser()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `merge()` function's use of `current.push(...next)` with a spread operator on a large `next` array to cause a stack overflow via `Function.prototype.apply` argument limit when `next` is an array with tens of thousands of elements, crashing the Node.js process during a partition event?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestHandler.js -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: requestQueryParser()] Can an unprivileged external user send a query string with a very large number of unique keys (each appearing once) to cause the `for...of Object.entries(parsedQueryString)` loop in `requestQueryParser` to iterate over thousands of entries, consuming significant CPU time per request and causing CPU exhaustion during a network partition when requests are queuing up?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestHandler.js -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: requestQueryParser()] Can an unprivileged external user send a query string where the same key appears in both lowercase and uppercase forms (e.g., `result=SUCCESS&RESULT=FAIL`), causing `canonicalizeValue` to be called for each, and then `merge()` to combine them, resulting in an array `['success', 'fail']` that passes the `qs.parse` `arrayLimit` check but creates ambiguous filter state that causes incorrect DB query construction during a partition recovery?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestHandler.js -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: canonicalizeValue()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request with `order` or `result` query parameters containing extremely long string values (e.g., 1MB strings), causing `lowerCaseQueryValue` (called via `canonicalizeValue`) to allocate large string objects for each request, and during a network partition where requests queue up, causing unbounded memory growth that leads to OOM?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestHandler.js -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: canonicalizeValue()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request where `order` is repeated 100 times with mixed-case values, causing `canonicalizeValue` to call `value.map((v) => canonicalizationFunc(v))` on a 100-element array, and then `merge()` to combine it with another 100-element array, creating a 200-element array that bypasses the `arrayLimit` check in `qs.parse` because the merging happens post-parse?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestHandler.js -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: requestQueryParser()] Can an unprivileged external user send a query string with keys containing Unicode characters that lowercase to the same ASCII key (via `key.toLowerCase()`), causing unexpected key collisions in `caseInsensitiveQueryString` and triggering `merge()` for keys that were not intended to be repeated, potentially bypassing parameter count validation?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestHandler.js -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: requestQueryParser()] Can an unprivileged external user send a query string with a key that is a JavaScript prototype property (e.g., `__proto__`, `constructor`, `toString`), causing `lowerKey in caseInsensitiveQueryString` to return `true` for prototype-inherited properties and triggering `merge()` on unexpected values, leading to prototype pollution of the `caseInsensitiveQueryString` object during a partition event?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestHandler.js -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: requestQueryParser()] Can an unprivileged external user send a query string with `__proto__[statusCode]=500` or similar prototype pollution payload that, after `qs.parse` processes it (qs has prototype pollution protections but `depth: 1` may affect this), causes `res.locals.statusCode` to be overridden to a non-200 value, causing all responses during a partition to return incorrect status codes?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestHandler.js -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: requestLogger()] Can an unprivileged external user send requests with crafted headers that cause `httpContext.middleware` (used before `requestLogger`) to fail to create a new async context, resulting in `httpContext.set(requestIdLabel, requestId)` writing to a shared global context, causing request ID leakage between concurrent requests during a partition event?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestHandler.js -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: requestLogger()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that `requestLogger` is registered via `app.useExt(requestLogger)` and is `async`, so that if `randomString(8)` rejects (e.g., due to crypto module failure under partition stress), the unhandled promise rejection propagates to `handleRejection` which only logs a warning, allowing the request to continue with no `requestIdLabel` set in `httpContext`, causing null reference errors in downstream logging?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestHandler.js -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: requestLogger()] Can an unprivileged external user flood the server with concurrent requests to exhaust the Node.js event loop during a DB network partition, causing `await randomString(8)` to queue indefinitely and block all subsequent middleware execution, resulting in a full service denial for all users?
